timeline.event: Born in Moscow as the second of seven children to Mikhail Andreyevich and Maria Fyodorovna.
Enrolls with his older brother Mikhail at the Chermak boarding school in Moscow and studies there for three years.
Mother Maria Fyodorovna dies of tuberculosis; sent by his father with his brother Mikhail to St. Petersburg to enter the Military Engineering-Technical University.
Passes the entrance exam for the Military Engineering-Technical University and undergoes military education.
His father is murdered by serfs on his estate.
Graduates from the military engineering school and is commissioned as a sub-lieutenant in the St. Petersburg Engineering Command.
Resigns his commission to become a writer and devote himself to literature.
Debuts in the literary world with his first work, Poor Folk, receiving high praise from critic Vissarion Belinsky.
Arrested by authorities after joining Mikhail Petrashevsky's circle and reading Belinsky's Letter to Gogol.
Completes a four-year prison sentence in a katorga in Omsk.
Marries Maria Isayeva.
Returns to St. Petersburg.
Publishes the magazine Vremya (Time) with his brother, serializing The Insulted and Humiliated and The House of the Dead.
Founds a new magazine, Epokha (Epoch), with his brother, but it fails.
Completes his masterpiece, Crime and Punishment.
Writes The Idiot and Demons while living abroad (especially in Dresden) before returning home.
Publishes The Adolescent, earning a substantial sum that lifts him out of poverty.
Completes his final masterpiece, the novel The Brothers Karamazov.
Dies of a ruptured pulmonary artery at the age of 60.
His remains are interred in the Alexander Nevsky Lavra cemetery in St. Petersburg.

keyAchievements.title: Poor Folk
keyAchievements.description: Debuted spectacularly in the literary world in 1846 with his first work, receiving high praise from critic Vissarion Belinsky as 'the next Gogol'.

keyAchievements.title: The House of the Dead
keyAchievements.description: A work published based on his experiences during his exile in Siberia.

keyAchievements.title: Crime and Punishment
keyAchievements.description: Completed this work, considered one of his masterpieces, in 1866.

keyAchievements.title: The Adolescent
keyAchievements.description: Published this work in 1874, earning a substantial amount of money that allowed him to escape poverty.

keyAchievements.title: The Brothers Karamazov
keyAchievements.description: Completed the manuscript for this novel, widely considered his final masterpiece, in 1880.

keyAchievements.title: A Writer's Diary
keyAchievements.description: Included literary horizons, political and social criticism, essays, and short stories, later becoming invaluable documentary material for Dostoevsky studies.

faq.question: What ideological changes did Dostoevsky undergo?
faq.answer: Through deep reading of the Bible and his encounters with the lives of convicts and commoners during his Siberian exile, he underwent an ideological shift from a socialist to a Christian humanitarian.

faq.question: What personal experiences influenced Dostoevsky's literary world?
faq.answer: His experience of political persecution ending in a commuted death sentence moments before execution, his exile in Siberia, his chronic epilepsy, and his gambling habit frequently appeared as important elements and heavily influenced his works.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Dostoevsky's novels?
faq.answer: His novels often feature characters with disparate and extreme psychologies, demonstrating an astounding understanding of the human psyche while sharply analyzing the political, social, and spiritual world of contemporary Russia.

faq.question: Is Dostoevsky related to existentialism?
faq.answer: Walter Kaufmann once described Dostoevsky's novella Notes from Underground as the 'best overture for existentialism ever written', and he is sometimes considered one of the founders of existentialism.

timeline.event: Born in Gwangseok-dong, Haeju-bu, Hwanghae-do as the eldest son of father Ahn Tae-hun and mother Jo Maria
Married Kim Ah-ryeo and had his first meeting with Kim Gu
His father Ahn Tae-hun converted to Catholicism
Converted to Catholicism at age 19 and received the baptismal name Thomas
Promoted Catholic missionary work and the establishment of a Catholic university
A dispute arose between his father Ahn Tae-hun and Seo Won-hun, a Qing Dynasty physician
Battered Seo Won-hun along with about 10 others including Lee Yong-il
Upon hearing the news of the Eulsa Treaty, he went to Shanghai for the movement to restore national sovereignty, but was refused cooperation and returned; his father Ahn Tae-hun passed away
After moving to Jinnampo, Pyongan-namdo, he managed a coal trading company before liquidating it and starting an education movement by establishing Samheung School

Took over Nampo Donui School and worked as a teacher; responded to the National Debt Repayment Movement and was appointed as the head of the Gwanseo branch of the National Debt Repayment Association; went to Primorsky Krai and joined the righteous army
Went into exile in Manchuria after the dissolution of the Korean Empire's army

Under Jeon Je-deok's command, as the Lieutenant General of the Korean Righteous Army, Special Independent Commander, and Commander of the A-ryeong District, he and Eom In-seop led about 100 subordinates across the Tumen River to infiltrate the country and launch a surprise attack on the Japanese garrison
Formed the Dongui Danjihoe (Society of Cut Fingers) with 11 like-minded comrades and cut off a joint of his left ring finger to make a blood pledge
Left Vladivostok and moved to Harbin
Assassinated Ito Hirobumi in Harbin and was arrested by the Russian army
Sentenced to death at the Kwantung Government-General District Court in Lushun, Kwantung Leased Territory
Met with the Chief Justice of the High Court and raised an objection to being tried as a murderer for his actions as a righteous army commander
Received the sacrament of penance from Father Joseph Wilhelm
Offered his final Mass officiated by Father Wilhelm
His execution was postponed and he left his last requests to his two younger brothers
Executed at 10:00 AM at the age of 32
Posthumously awarded the Order of Merit for National Foundation, Lieutenant General (later revised to the Republic of Korea Medal)

keyAchievements.title: Assassination of Ito Hirobumi
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the anti-Japanese independence movement by assassinating Japanese politician Ito Hirobumi in Harbin on October 26, 1909.

keyAchievements.title: Education Movement
keyAchievements.description: Strived for the movement to restore national sovereignty through education by establishing Samheung School and taking over Nampo Donui School.

keyAchievements.title: Righteous Army Activities
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the righteous army movement after moving to Primorsky Krai in 1907, and engaged in combat with the Japanese army as the Lieutenant General of the Korean Righteous Army in 1908.

keyAchievements.title: Formation of Dongui Danjihoe
keyAchievements.description: Formed the Dongui Danjihoe with 11 comrades in 1909 and cut off his left ring finger to strengthen his will for independence with a blood pledge.

keyAchievements.title: Literary Works
keyAchievements.description: Left an ideological legacy by writing the unfinished book "On Peace in the East" and the autobiography "The History of Ahn Eung-chil" while in prison.

keyAchievements.title: Leaving Calligraphy
keyAchievements.description: Left many calligraphic works while imprisoned in Lushun Prison, some of which have been designated as national treasures.

faq.question: When and where was Ahn Jung-geun born?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun was born on September 2, 1879, in Gwangseok-dong, Haeju-bu, Hwanghae-do, as the eldest son of father Ahn Tae-hun and mother Jo Maria.

faq.question: Whom did Ahn Jung-geun assassinate?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun assassinated Japanese politician Ito Hirobumi in Harbin on October 26, 1909.

faq.question: Why did Ahn Jung-geun assassinate Ito Hirobumi?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun assassinated Ito Hirobumi because he judged that Ito had committed 15 crimes, including the assassination of Empress Myeongseong, the signing of the Eulsa Treaty, and the dethronement of Emperor Gojong.

faq.question: When was Ahn Jung-geun executed?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun was executed on March 26, 1910, following the assassination of Ito Hirobumi.

faq.question: Where are Ahn Jung-geun's remains?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun's remains were buried in the prisoners' cemetery near Lushun Prison, but their exact location has not been found to this day.

faq.question: What are the major works of Ahn Jung-geun?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun wrote "On Peace in the East" and the autobiography "The History of Ahn Eung-chil" while in prison.

faq.question: What is Ahn Jung-geun's baptismal name?
faq.answer: Ahn Jung-geun's baptismal name is Thomas.

timeline.event: Born in Majae, Chobu-myeon, Gwangju-bu, Gyeonggi-do.
After his father Jeong Jae-won resigned from his government post and returned to his rural home, he named Jeong Yak-yong's childhood name Gwinong.
Married Pungsan Hong, the daughter of military official Hong Hwa-bo.
His father Jeong Jae-won became the assistant secretary of the Board of Taxation and rented a house in Seoul, where he met Lee Ga-hwan and Lee Seung-hun.
Participated in the Western Learning doctrine training session at Jueosa Temple and Cheonjinam Hermitage in Yangju, Gyeonggi-do, led by Gwon Cheol-sin.
Passed the Jeungwangsi examination celebrating the investiture of the Crown Prince and became a Saengwon (licentiate) through the Hoesi examination.
Heard an explanation of Catholic doctrines from Lee Byeok, the brother-in-law of his eldest brother Jeong Yak-hyeon.
The Myeongnyebang Incident occurred, where the Myeongnyebang community run by Lee Seung-hun was discovered and he was dragged to the Board of Punishments.
King Jeongjo defined Catholicism as an evil cult and issued a ban, and ordered the search and burning of Western Learning books related to Catholicism.
Passed the highest-level state examination at the age of 27 and entered government service.
When the Jinsan Incident occurred, where Yun Ji-chung held his mother's funeral in a Catholic manner in Jinsan, Jeolla-do, he was exiled to Haemi, Seosan, but was released after 11 days.
Participated in the design of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress and utilized the Geojunggi (traditional crane).
His father Jeong Jae-won passed away.
Lectured at Seonggyungwan and patrolled Yeoncheon, Saknyeong, etc. as a secret royal inspector of Gyeonggi-do.
Demoted to Geumjeong Chalbang (station master) due to the Eulmyo Persecution incident.
Due to suspicions raised by Busagwa Bak Jang-seol, Lee Seung-hun was demoted to Yesan, Lee Ga-hwan to Chungju Moksa, and Jeong Yak-yong to Hongju Geumjeong Chalbang.
While serving as the Third Minister of the Board of Punishments, he was impeached by his older brother Jeong Yak-jeon, so he submitted a "Jamyeongso" (petition of self-vindication) and resigned.
While living in retirement in his hometown, he heard the news of King Jeongjo's death and went up to Seoul.
Queen Dowager Jeongsun issued an order suppressing Catholicism and began a purge of the Namin faction.
Arrested and imprisoned, then his sentence was reduced to exile and he was released.
Began 18 years of exile in places like Janggi, Gyeongsang-do, and Gangjin, Jeolla-do.
His son Jeong Nong-jang (Nong-a) died of smallpox.
His exile was lifted and he rose to the position of Seungji (Royal Secretary).
Returned to his hometown.
While in exile in Gangjin-gun, Jeolla-do, he conceived the Jeongjeon-ron (well-field system) to improve land inequality.
Passed away at his home in Mahyeon-ri.
Posthumously promoted to Jeongheon Daebu Gyujanggak Jehak and granted the posthumous title 'Mundo'.

keyAchievements.title: Extensive Writing Activities
keyAchievements.description: Known as the person who wrote the most books since the invention of Chinese characters, authoring over 500 volumes.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Mokminsimseo"
keyAchievements.description: A book that details the principles and mindset of local magistrates who govern the people.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Heumheumsinseo"
keyAchievements.description: A book detailing the principles and norms of judgments, punishments, and prison administration based on actual cases he investigated while serving as the magistrate of Goksan.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Gyeongseyupyo"
keyAchievements.description: A book discussing appropriate and standard institutional regulations for state management.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Magwahoetong"
keyAchievements.description: Authored the measles treatment book "Magwahoetong" based on Lee Heon-gil's "Majingibang," saving the lives of countless Joseon people.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Geojunggi
keyAchievements.description: Created the pulley-and-windlass system for lifting heavy objects and invented the Geojunggi (traditional crane) for use during the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress.

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of Silhak
keyAchievements.description: Synthesized the Silhak (Practical Learning) ideology handed down through Yu Hyeong-won and Seongho Yi Ik into a comprehensive system.

keyAchievements.title: Conception of Jeongjeon-ron
keyAchievements.description: Conceived the Jeongjeon-ron (well-field system) to improve land inequality through the free distribution of land and communal labor-communal distribution.

keyAchievements.title: Selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure
keyAchievements.description: Selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure in 2012 to commemorate the 250th anniversary of his birth.

faq.question: When was Jeong Yak-yong born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong was born on August 5, 1762, and passed away on April 7, 1836.

faq.question: What are the major works of Jeong Yak-yong?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong's three major works are "Mokminsimseo", "Heumheumsinseo", and "Gyeongseyupyo".

faq.question: What was Jeong Yak-yong's relationship with Catholicism?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong was once deeply engrossed in Catholicism but apostatized after the Sinhae Persecution (1791), and actively participated in searching for Catholic believers during the Sinyu Persecution (1801).

faq.question: What did Jeong Yak-yong do during his exile?
faq.answer: During his 18-year exile, Jeong Yak-yong authored extensive writings, including "Mokminsimseo" and "Gyeongseyupyo".

faq.question: How did Jeong Yak-yong contribute to the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress?
faq.answer: During the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress, Jeong Yak-yong created a pulley-and-windlass system for lifting heavy objects and invented the Geojunggi (traditional crane) to utilize it.

faq.question: What did Jeong Yak-yong's ideology aim for?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong aimed to establish Silhak of statecraft and practical use, correct distorted theories by returning to the essence of Confucianism of Confucius and Mencius, and build a welfare state that cares for the socially and economically weak.

faq.question: Was Jeong Yak-yong selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure?
faq.answer: Yes, Jeong Yak-yong was selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure in 2012, marking the 250th anniversary of his birth.

timeline.event: Born in Tus, Khorasan.
Abandoned his prestigious positions due to religious anguish and other causes, and traveled to Mecca and various regions as a Sufi ascetic.
Retired to his hometown and passed away.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Incoherence of the Philosophers'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a book criticizing some philosophers for excessively transplanting Greek philosophy into Islam, holding thoughts that contradict the Quran.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Revival of the Religious Sciences' and 'Deliverance from Error'
keyAchievements.description: Left behind masterworks such as 'The Revival of the Religious Sciences' and 'Deliverance from Error' which had a great influence on future generations.

keyAchievements.title: Harmonization of orthodox theology and Sufism
keyAchievements.description: Made his greatest contribution by harmonizing orthodox theology and Sufism, breathing new life into the Islamic religious system.

keyAchievements.title: Defended orthodox Islam
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to adhering to orthodox Islam by criticizing Ibn Sina's philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Provided a turning point for Islamic scholarship
keyAchievements.description: Brought a turning point to Islamic religious thought by breaking away Islamic scholarship from scholastic theology and reviving the Quran and Hadith.

keyAchievements.title: Established the status of Sufism
keyAchievements.description: Established the status of Sufism within Islam, making the masses of Muslims accept Sufism.

faq.question: How is Al-Ghazali evaluated?
faq.answer: He is referred to as the greatest Muslim after Muhammad, and is a significant figure in the fields of theology, philosophy, and Sufism.

faq.question: What are Al-Ghazali's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include 'The Incoherence of the Philosophers', 'The Revival of the Religious Sciences', and 'Deliverance from Error'.

faq.question: What is Al-Ghazali's greatest contribution?
faq.answer: His greatest contribution is the harmonization of orthodox theology and Sufism, and the defense of orthodox Islam by criticizing the philosophy of Ibn Sina.

faq.question: What turning point did Al-Ghazali bring to Islamic religious thought?
faq.answer: He broke Islamic scholarship away from scholastic theology, reintroduced esoteric elements, established the status of Sufism, and made philosophy more accessible to ordinary Muslims.

timeline.event: Born
Created the masterpieces 'Inochi futatsu no naka ni ikitaru sakura kana' and 'Kumo oriori hito o yasumuru tsukimi kana'
Created the masterpiece 'Furuike ya kawazu tobikomu mizu no oto' (Old pond / a frog jumps in / sound of water)
Died (November 28, lunar October 12)

keyAchievements.title: Recognition of Haikai-no-renga style
keyAchievements.description: His works were recognized during his lifetime in the collaborative haikai-no-renga style.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Haiku
keyAchievements.description: Today, he is considered a master of haiku, which was called hokku during his time.

keyAchievements.title: Internationally renowned poet
keyAchievements.description: His poetry is internationally renowned, and many of his poems in Japan are reproduced at monuments or historical sites.

keyAchievements.title: Fame across Japan
keyAchievements.description: He entered the world of poetry at a young age, and by integrating himself into the intellectual landscape of Edo, he became famous throughout Japan.

keyAchievements.title: Inspiration through travel
keyAchievements.description: He wandered throughout Japan, including the north, east, and west, to gain inspiration for his works.

keyAchievements.title: Poetry based on experiences of nature
keyAchievements.description: His poetry was influenced by his direct experiences of the world around him, condensing emotions about landscapes into simple elements.

faq.question: Who was Matsuo Basho?
faq.answer: Matsuo Basho, born in 1644 and died on November 28, 1694 (lunar October 12), was a poet of the early Edo period. His childhood name was Kinsaku, and he was also known as Matsuo Chuemon Munefusa.

faq.question: What are Matsuo Basho's major achievements?
faq.answer: During his lifetime, his works were recognized in the collaborative haikai-no-renga style, and today he is considered a master of haiku. His poetry is internationally renowned and reproduced at monuments and historical sites across Japan.

faq.question: What are Matsuo Basho's representative works?
faq.answer: Representative works include 'Inochi futatsu no naka ni ikitaru sakura kana' and 'Kumo oriori hito o yasumuru tsukimi kana' from 1685, and 'Furuike ya kawazu tobikomu mizu no oto' from 1686.

faq.question: How did Matsuo Basho conduct his literary activities?
faq.answer: While making a living as a teacher, he distanced himself from the social and urban life where literary circles were based, wandering throughout Japan to gain inspiration. His poetry was influenced by direct experiences of the surrounding world, condensing his emotions about landscapes.

faq.question: Which of his works did Matsuo Basho consider most important?
faq.answer: He believed his best work lay in participating in and leading renku, and considered the connection with haikai verses more important than hokku.

timeline.event: Born into a Jewish family in Erlangen, Bavaria, Germany.
Enrolled as a mathematics student when the University of Erlangen allowed women to matriculate.
Received her doctorate under Paul Gordan.
Moved to Göttingen, Germany.
Appointed to a teaching position at the University of Göttingen.
Published the paper 'Idealtheorie in Ringbereichen' (Theory of Ideals in Ring Domains).
Published the paper 'Abstrakter Aufbau der Idealtheorie in algebraischen Zahl- und Funktionenkörpern' (Abstract Structure of the Theory of Ideals in Algebraic Number and Function Fields).
Fled Germany and became a professor at Bryn Mawr College in the US after Nazi racial laws barred her from university teaching.
Died on April 14.

keyAchievements.title: Noetherian Ring
keyAchievements.description: Established the concept of calling a ring where all ideals are finitely generated a Noetherian ring.

keyAchievements.title: Lasker-Noether Theorem
keyAchievements.description: Presented a major result in commutative algebra regarding the primary decomposition of ideals.

keyAchievements.title: Noether's Theorem
keyAchievements.description: Published a fundamental theorem in mathematical physics that establishes a one-to-one correspondence between symmetries and conservation laws.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of Central Simple Algebras
keyAchievements.description: Established the theory of central simple algebras, alongside Emil Artin and Helmut Hasse.

keyAchievements.title: 'Idealtheorie in Ringbereichen'
keyAchievements.description: In this paper, she analyzed the ascending chain condition on ideals.

keyAchievements.title: 'Abstrakter Aufbau der Idealtheorie in algebraischen Zahl- und Funktionenkörpern'
keyAchievements.description: In this paper, she proved that commutative rings in which unique prime factorization of ideals holds are Dedekind domains and proved the isomorphism theorems for rings.

faq.question: Where was Emmy Noether born?
faq.answer: Emmy Noether was born into a Jewish family in Erlangen, Bavaria, Germany.

faq.question: What was Emmy Noether's main field of research?
faq.answer: Emmy Noether primarily researched algebra, leaving behind many achievements such as Noetherian rings and the Lasker-Noether theorem.

faq.question: When did Emmy Noether receive her PhD?
faq.answer: Emmy Noether received her PhD in 1907 under the supervision of Paul Gordan.

faq.question: Why did Emmy Noether leave Germany?
faq.answer: When the Nazi regime's racial laws banned her from university teaching in 1933, she fled Germany and emigrated to the United States.

faq.question: What is Noether's theorem about?
faq.answer: Noether's theorem is a profound achievement in mathematical physics that demonstrates a one-to-one correspondence between symmetries and conservation laws.

timeline.event: Born in Alexandria in Roman Egypt.
Attended the First Council of Nicaea as a deacon, playing a key role in the movement against Arianism.
Succeeded Alexander to become the Bishop (or Pope) of Alexandria.
Sent into his first exile to Trier in Gaul by Emperor Constantine I.
Sent into his second exile to Rome under Emperor Constantius II.
Third exile. Fled to the Egyptian desert and lived among the Desert Fathers.
Sent into his fourth exile under Emperor Julian the Apostate.
Sent into his fifth and final exile under Emperor Valens.
Died peacefully in Alexandria after a life of conflict and theological apologetics.

keyAchievements.title: Defender of Nicene Orthodoxy
keyAchievements.description: He was the foremost defender of the divinity of Christ against the widespread Arian heresy, ensuring the survival of the Nicene Creed.

keyAchievements.title: Author of 'On the Incarnation'
keyAchievements.description: This work is a foundational text of Christian theology, explaining why God became human in Jesus Christ to save humanity.

keyAchievements.title: Biographer of Anthony the Great
keyAchievements.description: His 'Life of Antony' became a bestseller of its time, popularizing the ideals of monasticism throughout the Roman Empire.

faq.question: What is Arianism?
faq.answer: Arianism was a major 4th-century theological doctrine which taught that Jesus Christ was a created being, subordinate to God the Father, and not co-eternal or of the same substance (homoousios) as the Father.

faq.question: Why was he called 'Athanasius contra mundum'?
faq.answer: This Latin phrase means 'Athanasius against the world'. It reflects how he stood virtually alone in his steadfast opposition to Arianism during a time when Roman Emperors and the majority of bishops supported it.

faq.question: How many times was Athanasius exiled?
faq.answer: During his 45 years as bishop, he was exiled five times by four different Roman Emperors, spending over 17 years away from his diocese in Alexandria.
